article_meta.title: Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell on June 25, 2025, in Washington, DC

HEADLINE: Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell on June 25, 2025, in Washington, DC
SOURCE: CNN (Opinion)

ARTICLE:

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell on June 25, 2025, in Washington, DC. Kent Nishimura/Getty Images Donald Trump Federal agencies Interest rates Investing See all topics Facebook Tweet Email Link Link Copied! Follow Washington вҖ” The gloves are off in the heated clash between Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell and President Donald Trump. As extraordinary as a criminal investigation of a sitting Fed chair is, PowellГўВҖВҷs rebuke of Trump was in some ways even more shocking. For years, Powell has remained mum even as Trump called him every name and used every insult in the book, repeatedly threatening to fire him. PowellГўВҖВҷs playbook has been to remain neutral, saying his one and only focus was on the FedГўВҖВҷs politically independent mission to maintain maximum employment and low inflation. Everything else was a distraction, Powell maintained. Trump forced PowellГўВҖВҷs hand after the Justice Department served him with subpoenas. But in PowellГўВҖВҷs remarkable show of defiance, he didnГўВҖВҷt focus on his own actions but on defending the Fed against the Trump administrationГўВҖВҷs attack on its independence ГўВҖВ” and, more broadly, the US economy. In his stunning two-minute video Sunday night, Powell characterized the Trump administrationГўВҖВҷs potential prosecution as a threat against the livelihoods of the American public to serve TrumpГўВҖВҷs whims. ГҜ"ВҝГўВҖВңThe threat of criminal charges is a consequence of the Federal Reserve setting interest rates based on our best assessment of what will serve the public, rather than following the preferences of the president,ГўВҖВқ Powell said. ГўВҖВңThis is about whether the Fed will be able to continue to set interest rates based on evidence and economic conditions ГўВҖВ” or whether instead monetary policy will be directed by political pressure or intimidation.ГўВҖВқ The fact that Powell spoke out forcefully against the Trump administration this week for the first time is all the more notable because Powell has long treasured and promoted the FedГўВҖВҷs independence, which he sees as tantamount to its mission to help American households live better lives. It enables the Fed to do its core mission: making jobs plentiful and keeping prices in check, even when politically imprudent. An emboldened Powell Economists, academics and former Fed officials broadly agree that safeguarding Fed independence is necessary for the soundness of the US economy. It ensures policymakers can make tough decisions on interest rates that are rooted in economic reality and not the whims of whoever controls the White House. For example, a president may want lower rates to grow the economy and send stocks higher, but that policy could hurt Americans by sending prices out of control. Powell has been an ardent defender of the FedГўВҖВҷs ability to set interest rates free of politics throughout TrumpГўВҖВҷs relentless pressure campaign over the past year. For example, during a press conference in July, Powell was asked about TrumpГўВҖВҷs recent attacks on him and the Fed. Powell didnГўВҖВҷt engage ГўВҖВ“ but offered a long defense of the FedГўВҖВҷs independence. ГўВҖВңIГўВҖВҷll just say that, so I think that having an independent central bank has been an institutional arrangement that has served the public well. And as long as it serves the public well, it should continue and be respected. If it didnГўВҖВҷt serve the public well, then it wouldnГўВҖВҷt be something that we should just automatically defend,ГўВҖВқ Powell said. ГўВҖВңBut what it gives us and other central banks, what it gives you is the ability to make these very challenging decisions that in ways that are focused on the data and the evolving outlook, the balance of risks and all of things we talk about, and not political factors.ГўВҖВқ Trump noted that the Fed is hardly alone ГўВҖВ“ governments of advanced economies across the world have put distance between monetary policymakers and politicians. ГўВҖВңIf you were not to have that, that would be a great temptation, of course, to use interest rates to affect elections, for example,ГўВҖВқ Powell added. ГўВҖВңAnd thatГўВҖВҷs something that we donГўВҖВҷt want to do.ГўВҖВқ An exterior view of the Marriner S. Eccles Federal Reserve building on November 29, 2025, in Washington, DC. Aaron M. Sprecher/AP Previously, the closest Powell had come to criticizing Trump was in November 2024, when he was asked whether he would leave his post early if Trump demanded it. ГўВҖВңNo,ГўВҖВқ Powell said in a press conference. Asked to elaborate, Powell said, ГўВҖВңNot permitted under the law.ГўВҖВқ Why the about face? Because Powell viewed the potential prosecution as an existential threat that could forever politicize the Fed, undermine its credibility and upset global markets and the economy. ThatГўВҖВҷs part of the reason central bankers from around the world rallied to PowellГўВҖВҷs defense Tuesday. In the statement, the worldГўВҖВҷs central bankers stressed that the independence of central banks is a ГўВҖВңcornerstone of price, financial and economic stabilityГўВҖВқ and that protecting that independence with full respect for the law is critical. The statement praised Powell for serving with ГўВҖВңintegrityГўВҖВқ and for his ГўВҖВңunwavering commitment to the public interest.ГўВҖВқ Fed remains independent, for now Among the many problems with TrumpГўВҖВҷs demands for the Fed to lower rates is the perception of political interference when the Fed does take that action. The Fed in the second half of last year lowered interest rates three times in a row, though Trump has asked for more. Trump has said the United States should have rates that are among the lowest in the world and that he should have a say in rate decisions. To avoid the perception of influence, some Fed observers believe the central bank could hold off on rate cuts for longer. In other words, ridding the Fed of its independence could backfire spectacularly on Trump by making further rate cuts less likely and even emboldening Powell to remain on the FedГўВҖВҷs board after his term as chair ends on May 15. He is serving a concurrent term on the board that runs through 2028. ГўВҖВңMy thought is that I really want to turn this job over to whoever replaces me in ГўВҖВ” with the economy in really good shape. ThatГўВҖВҷs what I want to do,ГўВҖВқ Powell said in December after the Fed announced its latest rate cut, in response to a question posed by CNNГўВҖВҷs Matt Egan on what Powell hopes his legacy as chair will be. The Trump administration also has a tough case to prove against Powell. Investors and economists were puzzled by the latest salvo in the Trump-Powell fight, considering PowellГўВҖВҷs term as chair ends in just four months. The federal investigation also isnГўВҖВҷt sitting well with a growing number of Republicans in Congress, such as Sens. Thom Tillis of North Carolina and Lisa Murkowski of Alaska, along with Rep. French Hill of Arkansas. ГўВҖВңPeople that respond publicly to an investigation, outwardly and firmly, like Powell, are usually in the driverГўВҖВҷs seat,ГўВҖВқ Evan Gotlob, a former federal prosecutor, told CNN. Trump allies have accused Powell of mismanaging the central bankГўВҖВҷs renovation, which became more costly in recent years, and of lying under oath when he described some of the upgrades to senators in June 2025. Peter Navarro, a senior counselor to the president, told Fox Business on Monday: ГўВҖВңWe know he made false statements.ГўВҖВқ ГўВҖВңThe only question is whether he was aware and did it with malice,ГўВҖВқ he said. The Fed has been forthcoming about the renovationГўВҖВҷs cost overruns and its collaboration with other agencies, even posting an FAQ on its website with all those details. The Fed last summer also provided a guided tour of the renovation to a handful of reporters. ГўВҖВңLying to Congress is a tough thing to prove,ГўВҖВқ former federal prosecutor Gotlob said. ГўВҖВңPowell is definitely in a better position than the Justice Department.ГўВҖВқ CNNГўВҖВҷs Matt Egan contributed reporting. Donald Trump Federal agencies Interest rates Investing See all topics Facebook Tweet Email Link Link Copied! Follow
---

parsed_output.reasoning: The article frames Donald Trump and his administration's actions toward the Federal Reserve and Jerome Powell in a critical light, repeatedly defending the institution's independence and portraying Trump's attempts as possibly damaging, extreme, or self-serving. It emphasizes international and bipartisan defense of Powell, and treats his defiance as praiseworthy, which is consistent with center-left to left-leaning media framing. While factual, the narrative framing, selection of quotes, and value judgments demonstrate a lean against Trump. CNN Opinion pieces are typically more liberal, which corroborates the observed bias.
